nativeRange.habitat: Sandy lagoons, coastal flats, and reef-adjacent shallows at 2–25 m depth, typically in association with a host sea anemone (most commonly Stichodactyla haddoni in the wild)

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied omnivorous diet. Has been documented eating small invertebrates and algae in the wild. In aquaria, marine flake and pellet foods, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, chopped table shrimp, and herbivore preparations including spirulina are commonly fed once or twice daily.

avoidWith: other Amphiprion sebae individuals (unless mated pair)

summary: The Sebae Clownfish (Amphiprion sebae) is an Indian Ocean anemonefish, easily confused with Clarkii. Generally a hardy beginner-friendly species; tends to be semi-aggressive and benefits from a host anemone, classically Stichodactyla haddoni.
careNotes:
The Sebae Clownfish (Amphiprion sebae) is an Indian Ocean member of the saddleback clownfish complex. Adults are typically black with two wide white body bars and a yellow or orange face, with a yellow-to-white caudal fin and yellow anal fin.

Misidentification is extremely common in the trade. Many fish sold as "Sebae Clownfish" are actually Indian Ocean morphs of Amphiprion clarkii. The true A. sebae is distinguished by a mostly white-and-black soft dorsal fin (versus fully yellow in Clarkii), absence of a third white stripe on the caudal peduncle, and association with sandy lagoon habitat rather than rockwork. The common name "sebae anemone" historically applied to Heteractis crispa is also a source of confusion — H. crispa is not the primary natural host of A. sebae.

Like all clownfish, A. sebae is protandrous: all individuals are born male and the dominant fish in a pair becomes female. (This is the opposite of protogynous, female-first change.) Pairs are best established by introducing both fish simultaneously when small, or by purchasing a confirmed mated pair. A. sebae has been known to be more aggressive toward conspecifics and other clownfish species than Ocellaris or Percula and is generally best kept singly or as a single mated pair per tank.

Natural host anemone in the wild is most commonly Haddon's Anemone (Stichodactyla haddoni); also reported with Heteractis aurora, Heteractis malu, and occasionally Macrodactyla doreensis. In aquaria, Bubbletip Anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) and other Heteractis species are also accepted. A host anemone is not strictly required for captive survival.

Water parameter notes: Sources varied meaningfully on temperature. LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase's environmental model gives 27.3–29.3°C (notably warmer, reflecting tropical Indian Ocean water column means), and Reeflings cites 24–28°C. Averaged values (24.5–27.5°C) were used. pH ranged 8.0–8.5 across sources (averaged 8.0–8.4). Salinity ranged SG 1.020–1.026; averaged to SG 1.021–1.026.

Adult size: FishBase records 16.0 cm TL maximum, LiveAquaria lists 6 inches (15.24 cm), Reeflings 12 cm. These are consistent; averaged max ≈ 14.4 cm. Most aquarium specimens settle around 10–13 cm at maturity. Tank minimum: LiveAquaria 30 gallons (~114 L) and Reeflings 100 L are consistent; rounded up to a nice 120 L to allow for the sandy footprint and host-anemone real estate this species favors.
breedingNotes: Amphiprion sebae has been reported to breed readily in home aquariums, though somewhat less commonly than Ocellaris or Percula. Females are the larger of a pair. Pairs typically deposit eggs on a flat, cleaned surface near the host anemone or another protected site; both parents guard and aerate the clutch, with the male doing most of the tending. Eggs have been reported to hatch in roughly 6–11 days depending on water temperature. Larval rearing is the primary bottleneck: newly hatched fry require dedicated rearing tanks and a first food of rotifers, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as they grow.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Direct product page for True Sebae Clownfish, Captive-Bred. Provided adult size (6 in / 15.24 cm), temperature (72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), minimum tank (30 gal / ~114 L), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), diet (Omnivore), reef safe (Yes), family (Pomacentridae), Bubbletip Anemone as listed host, and breeding notes (6–11 day egg incubation). Reached via the /collections/clownfish landing page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum adult size (16.0 cm TL), environmental temperature range (27.3–29.3°C, mean 28.8°C — notably warmer than aquarium-trade recommendations), depth range (2–25 m), distribution (Indian Ocean: Arabian Peninsula, India, Sri Lanka, Maldives, Andaman Islands, Sumatra, Java), habitat (coastal waters and lagoons), taxonomy (Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria), trophic level 2.7, IUCN Least Concern (2021), reproductive mode (oviparous, demersal adhesive eggs, paternal guarding), and host anemone (Stichodactyla haddoni).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.0–8.5), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026), minimum tank (~100 L / 26 gal), care level (easy), temperament (generally peaceful but territorial around anemones), reef safe (yes), lifespan (10–15 years in captivity), host anemones (H. crispa, H. magnifica, E. quadricolor in captivity), and protandrous breeding biology with 6–11 day egg incubation. Used to inform parameter averaging.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Used for identification and distribution context. Documented frequent misidentification of Indian Ocean A. clarkii morphs as A. sebae, distinguishing features (white-and-black soft dorsal, lack of third caudal-peduncle stripe), natural host anemones (Stichodactyla haddoni primary; Heteractis aurora, H. malu, Macrodactyla doreensis), and Indian Ocean endemic range from the Persian Gulf to Bali. BlueZoo Aquatics product search 404'd; Quality Marine was substituted as an additional verification source.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs have been reported to spawn readily on flat surfaces near a host anemone in home aquariums, with eggs hatching in roughly 6–11 days. Larval rearing is the primary challenge and requires a dedicated rearing tank, rotifers as first food, and a transition to baby brine shrimp.
